mnemonics: Gradient Descent's Goal: Go Downhill (GDDG) - The gradient of MSE points downhill, so we go the opposite way (negative gradient) to minimize error and reach the valley of lowest loss.
keyPoints: The gradient of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function points in the direction of the steepest increase in error.
To minimize the error, an optimization algorithm (like Gradient Descent) moves in the opposite direction of this gradient.
Computing this gradient is fundamental for updating model parameters in machine learning and leads to the famous 'normal equations' in linear regression.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the factor of 2 (or 1/n) that comes from differentiating the square, leading to incorrect update steps that are off by a constant factor.
Confusing the gradient of MSE (a vector for multi-parameter models) with the derivative of a simple quadratic function (a scalar for single-parameter models).
Misinterpreting the gradient's direction and accidentally moving towards the maximum (increasing error) instead of the minimum by forgetting to use the negative gradient.
